steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank fills in the phrase "keen interest to ______ of Leiden University." The slot contains a title (linguist) followed directly by a proper name (Marian Klamer) that identifies the specific person. The question tests whether punctuation is needed between the title and the name.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘linguist, Marian Klamer’: a comma after "linguist" suggests the name is added parenthetically, but the phrase has no closing comma and becomes grammatically unbalanced. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘linguist, Marian Klamer,’: commas on both sides treat "Marian Klamer" as nonrestrictive—but it is not an aside; it identifies which linguist, so no commas are correct. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘linguist Marian Klamer’: no punctuation between the title and the identifying name is standard when a proper name directly follows a title to specify the person. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘linguist: Marian Klamer’: a colon introduces a list or explanation; it cannot link a title to the name it belongs to. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
